claims.claim: In a synthetic 3-class heteroskedastic classification task replicated across 10 seeds at 12,000 samples each, UCCE consistently reallocates the uncertainty budget toward hard examples: under distribution shift, marginal coverage improved from 0.8721 to 0.8891 and coverage error roughly halved (0.0279 → 0.0146).

evidence_refs.quote: Split-conformal prediction provides finite-sample marginal coverage guarantees but can exhibit substantial conditional undercoverage in heterogeneous or shifted data regions. We investigate a post-hoc Uncertainty-Coverage Co-Estimator (UCCE) that uses a gradient-boosted local quantile regressor to predict per-example nonconformity thresholds from classifier features, then applies a residual split-conformal correction on a held-out calibration split. claims.claim: The baseline method (`global_split_conformal`) computes a single nonconformity threshold from a calibration split using the score $s_i = 1 - p_{\text{true}}(x_i)$, where $p_{\text{true}}$ is the classifier's estimated probability for the true class.

claims.claim: The threshold is set at the $\lceil (n+1)(1-\alpha) \rceil / n$ quantile of calibration scores, yielding prediction sets with marginal coverage at level $1 - \alpha$ under exchangeability.
